questions.question: A message broker sits between producers and consumers so they never call each other directly. Which benefit specifically describes the broker holding a backlog while the consumer is down or slow?
options: Temporal decoupling
Independent scaling
Routing flexibility
Strong consistency
questions.explanation: Temporal decoupling means sender and receiver need not be up at the same time; the broker holds the backlog while the consumer is down. Independent scaling is about adding consumers to drain faster, not about absorbing downtime.

questions.question: In point-to-point (queue) versus publish/subscribe (topic), how many consumers receive a single given message in each model?
options: Queue: exactly one consumer; topic: every interested subscriber
Queue: every consumer; topic: exactly one subscriber
Queue: exactly one consumer; topic: exactly one subscriber
Queue: every consumer; topic: every subscriber
questions.explanation: A queue delivers each message to exactly one consumer (competing consumers share the load), while a topic delivers a copy to every interested subscriber (fan-out / broadcast).

questions.question: The word **topic** means different things across brokers. Which statement is correct?
options: In RabbitMQ a topic exchange is a wildcard-routing strategy and is still queue-based, not inherent pub/sub
In Kafka a topic alone provides fan-out, so no consumer groups are needed at all
In Service Bus a topic is just a named log that consumers position into via offsets
In RabbitMQ a topic is the pub/sub primitive where one topic maps to many subscriptions
questions.explanation: A RabbitMQ topic exchange is a wildcard routing strategy on routing keys and remains queue-based. Kafka fan-out comes from multiple consumer groups (not the topic itself), and the 'topic = subscriptions' primitive is Service Bus, not RabbitMQ.

questions.question: Adding competing consumers to a single queue scales throughput but, by default, sacrifices message ordering because parallel consumers finish out of order.
questions.explanation: Competing consumers process concurrently and finish out of order, so ordering is lost by default; preserving order requires partitioning/sessions, which limits parallelism to one consumer per key.

questions.question: With competing consumers, what is the risk of setting **prefetch / max-concurrent-calls** too high?
options: One consumer hoards in-flight messages, starving others and risking redelivery storms
The broker stops delivering until acks arrive, halving throughput
Messages are silently dropped before reaching any consumer
Ordering is automatically enforced, eliminating parallelism
questions.explanation: Too-high prefetch lets one consumer hold many in-flight messages, starving other consumers and risking redelivery storms if it falls behind; too-low underutilizes consumers. Messages are not dropped and ordering is not enforced by prefetch.

questions.question: In RabbitMQ, which exchange type ignores the routing key entirely and copies the message to every bound queue?
options: fanout
direct
topic
headers
questions.explanation: A fanout exchange ignores the routing key and broadcasts a copy to every bound queue. Direct does exact routing-key matching, topic does wildcard matching, and headers routes on header attributes.

questions.question: In a RabbitMQ topic exchange, what do the wildcards `*` and `#` match in a dotted routing key?
options: `*` matches exactly one word; `#` matches zero or more words
`*` matches zero or more words; `#` matches exactly one word
`*` matches one or more words; `#` matches exactly one word
Both match exactly one word, differing only in case sensitivity
questions.explanation: In a topic exchange `*` matches exactly one word and `#` matches zero or more words (e.g. `order.*.created`, `logs.#`).

questions.question: To guarantee a RabbitMQ message survives a broker restart, which conditions must ALL hold? (Select all that apply.)
options: The queue is declared durable
The message is published as persistent
Publishes are confirmed via publisher confirms
Consumers use auto-ack so messages settle immediately
The exchange is a fanout exchange
questions.explanation: Survival requires all three: durable queue, persistent message, and publisher confirms — miss any one and you can still lose messages on restart. Auto-ack actually causes loss on consumer crash, and the exchange type is irrelevant to durability.

questions.question: Given this RabbitMQ consumer, what delivery guarantee does it provide and what happens to a message whose `Handle` throws?
options: At-least-once; a throwing message is nacked with requeue=false and dead-lettered
At-most-once; a throwing message is silently discarded with no DLQ
Exactly-once; the broker deduplicates the failed message automatically
At-least-once; a throwing message is requeued to the same queue and retried forever
questions.explanation: `autoAck:false` with ack-after-processing is at-least-once. On failure `BasicNack` with `requeue:false` sends the message to the dead-letter exchange rather than requeuing it. There is no broker-side exactly-once dedup here.

questions.question: On Azure Service Bus, why is **Peek-Lock** the at-least-once mode while **Receive-and-Delete** is at-most-once?
options: Peek-Lock redelivers if a crash occurs before Complete; Receive-and-Delete removes on receive, so a crash loses it
Peek-Lock writes the message to three replicas before delivery; Receive-and-Delete writes to only one
Peek-Lock deduplicates on MessageId automatically; Receive-and-Delete disables duplicate detection
Peek-Lock commits offsets after processing; Receive-and-Delete commits offsets before processing
questions.explanation: Peek-Lock holds a lock until you Complete; a crash before Complete lets the lock expire and the message is redelivered (at-least-once). Receive-and-Delete removes the message on receive, so a crash loses it (at-most-once). Offsets are a Kafka concept, not Service Bus.

questions.question: You need strict FIFO ordering per customer on Azure Service Bus while still processing thousands of customers concurrently. Which feature achieves this?
options: Sessions: messages sharing a SessionId go in order to one consumer, while different sessions run in parallel
Duplicate detection: the broker drops repeated MessageIds within a window, enforcing order
Receive-and-Delete mode: removing on receive guarantees ordered processing
A single fanout exchange that copies messages to every subscription in order
questions.explanation: Sessions tie all messages with the same SessionId to one consumer in order, while different sessions run in parallel — ordered per key, scalable across keys. Duplicate detection prevents dups, not ordering, and exchanges are a RabbitMQ concept.

questions.question: On Azure Service Bus, messages that exceed **MaxDeliveryCount** or expire (TTL) are automatically moved to the entity's built-in dead-letter queue.
questions.explanation: Service Bus has a built-in DLQ: messages exceeding MaxDeliveryCount or expiring via TTL land in the entity's $DeadLetterQueue automatically, unlike Kafka which requires DIY poison-message handling.

questions.question: You must process payments per customer strictly in order while handling thousands of customers concurrently on Kafka. What is the correct design and its parallelism ceiling?
options: Use `customerId` as the partition key; ordering holds per partition and parallelism is capped by partition count
Use a random partition key; ordering holds globally and parallelism is capped by customer count
Use one partition for all customers; ordering holds globally and parallelism is capped by consumer count
Disable keys and rely on a single consumer group to reorder messages by timestamp
questions.explanation: Keying by `customerId` sends each customer's events to the same partition (ordered) while different customers spread across partitions for parallelism. Parallelism in a group is capped by partition count (at most one consumer per partition), never by customer count.

questions.question: A Kafka topic has 4 partitions and one consumer group with 6 consumers. What happens?
options: 4 consumers each own one partition; the other 2 sit idle
All 6 consumers share each partition's load round-robin
Each partition is read by 2 consumers for redundancy
Kafka rejects the group because consumers outnumber partitions
questions.explanation: Within a consumer group there is at most one consumer per partition, so with 4 partitions only 4 consumers get work and the remaining 2 sit idle. Partition count caps parallelism for a group.

questions.question: Why can Kafka replay already-consumed history while RabbitMQ and Service Bus cannot?
options: Kafka retains records regardless of consumption and consumers track offsets, so a group can seek backward
Kafka writes every message to a backup DLQ that the others lack, enabling re-reads
Kafka keeps a per-consumer copy until all consumer groups have acked the record
RabbitMQ and Service Bus retain messages forever but lack a seek API to reach them
questions.explanation: Kafka's broker is 'dumb': it retains records for the retention window regardless of who read them, and consumers track their own offsets, so you can seek to an earlier offset. RabbitMQ/Service Bus delete a message once it is acked/completed, leaving nothing to replay.

questions.question: A teammate says their pipeline is 'exactly-once' because they enabled Kafka idempotent producers and transactions — but it also writes rows to SQL Server. Is the end-to-end claim valid, and why?
options: No — Kafka EOS is exactly-once only within Kafka; the external SQL write must itself be made idempotent
Yes — idempotent producers extend exactly-once to any sink the producer writes to, including SQL Server
Yes — `isolation.level=read_committed` on the consumer guarantees the SQL write commits exactly once
No — but the fix is to disable idempotent producers, restoring exactly-once across the database boundary
questions.explanation: Kafka EOS (idempotence + transactions + read_committed) is exactly-once within Kafka only. An external SQL write is outside that transaction, so end-to-end you have at-least-once and must make the SQL write idempotent (e.g. dedup/upsert by message id). Disabling idempotence would make things worse, not better.

questions.question: A consumer acks/commits **before** processing the message. Which guarantee is this, and what is its failure mode?
options: At-most-once; a crash after the ack but before processing completes loses the message
At-least-once; a crash causes the message to be redelivered as a duplicate
Exactly-once; the early ack plus broker dedup prevents both loss and duplication
At-least-once; the early ack forces the broker to retain the message until processing finishes
questions.explanation: Acking before processing is at-most-once: if the consumer crashes after the ack but before finishing, the message is gone (lost) with no redelivery. At-least-once requires acking after processing; broker dedup does not turn an early ack into exactly-once.

questions.question: Consider this inbox-dedup pattern. Which statements about it are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: Inserting the MessageId and applying the effect in the SAME transaction is what makes the dedup atomic with the side effect
On a duplicate, the primary-key violation on ProcessedMessages aborts the transaction before the UPDATE takes effect
This makes an at-least-once consumer effectively-once for this effect
Broker-side duplicate detection (e.g. Service Bus dedup) alone removes the need for this inbox table end-to-end
The UPDATE must run before the INSERT for the dedup to short-circuit duplicates
questions.explanation: Recording the MessageId in the same transaction as the effect makes them atomic; a PK collision on a duplicate aborts the whole transaction before any effect, giving effectively-once. Broker-side dedup covers producer-side dups only, not consumer reprocessing, so the inbox is still needed; and the INSERT must precede/accompany the effect to short-circuit, not follow it.
